data.title: MSDL And BXSL: High Yields Won't Save You From Negative Total Returns
data.description:
Investment thesis

The initial purpose of my article on the assets of the BDC Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL) and Blackstone Secured Lending Fund (BXSL) is to make a direct comparison to determine whether either of them is worthy of inclusion in my portfolio. The final results of the fundamental analysis, though, show some negative stuff in how their risk-reward balance might change going forward. The current cycle of Fed rate cuts is hurting those BDC companies with the highest share of floating interest rates. And MSDL and BXSL have it at almost 100%. Given the current discount on the P/NAV metric, the value of their shares is trading 22% and 6% lower. At this time, I do not recommend buying these assets, expecting a continuation of the negative dynamics in their Total Return indicators.

Fundamental similarities and differences between MSDL and BXSL

Despite the fact that MSDL and BXSL have different management scales, there is an interesting dilemma between the "gold standard" of the BDC segment (in terms of portfolio risk assessment) and potentially undervalued assets (after all, both are trading at a discount to NAV). A number of qualities related to investment strategy and management structure make them similar:

  • Both companies are backed by global financial players (Morgan Stanley and Blackstone), both of which provide access to priority deals in the BDC category. These factors reduce the cost of customer acquisition and servicing. In addition, both companies are able to lend to large middle-market businesses, with special SEC approval allowing them to participate in co-investment programs in large credit lines involving their parent companies.
  • And since the parent companies are focused on credit portfolio security, MSDL and BXSL have adopted this strategy. Both adhere to a defensive strategy, granting loans to the most secure entities capable of securing loans with first lien rights. This means that in the event of a potential borrower default, the BDCs are the first to claim compensation from the assets sold.
  • These companies' credit policies are geared toward ensuring that the maximum share of their portfolios consists of floating interest rates. It means that their net income is correlated with the SOFR rate, currently at 3.66%. During periods of tightening monetary policy by the Fed, such a feature allowed them to protect themselves from rising costs. But during periods of Fed rate cuts, it is the main risk for reducing BDC margins.
  • Their high-quality management keeps the share of non-performing loans in their portfolios extremely low. It reflects their discipline in risk assessment. Also, they are known for their low base fees (at 1.0%). It makes them focused on scaling their operations rather than squeezing profits out of investors' assets.

In spite of these similarities, there are a number of differences between MSDL and BXSL that are important to mention. They can be divided into three groups: asset quality, operating efficiency with dividends, and borrowing costs.

Asset quality.

The quality of assets affects the level of credit risk. Neither company is particularly risky in this regard, though there is a difference in their more specialized customer focus. The MSDL prefers to work with a slightly smaller segment of small businesses than the BXSL. As a result, it currently has a non-accruals ratio of 1.2%. That's still a great number, though investors are more nervous about the decline in net asset value throughout 2025. At BSXL, the same non-accruals ratio is 0.1%. This is the benchmark for stability in credit risk assessment. The BXSL specializes in working with large medium-sized businesses with a minimum annual EBITDA of $100 million. As a result, the BXSL's borrowers are more economically mature and therefore more financially reliable. If there is an economic storm or systemic crisis in the economy, BXSL's portfolio will be more resilient than MSDL's.

Operational efficiency with dividends.

Differences in operational efficiency affect companies' dividend policy prospects. This is because the amount of net investment income per share determines the level of regular dividend coverage. For MSDL, it is close to 100%, whereas BXSL has a small margin of safety at 106% (data for Q3 2025). But it's going down. In Q3 2024, for example, the figure was 118%, and a year earlier it was 123%. This is a negative trend and signals a potential reduction in dividends in the future. Should MSDL's operational efficiency decline, the dividend coverage ratio will fall below the required level of 100%. After that, the question of reducing the dividends themselves will become relevant. In my opinion, that is the main reason why MSDL shares are trading at such a high discount to the P/NAV metric (0.78x).

Cost of borrowing.

MSDL and BXSL have roughly equal debt-to-equity (leverage) ratios (1.17x and 1.15x). In comparison with the median for the BDC sector, this is a healthy level that does not signal any danger for the company. Analyzing BXSL, note that this company aims to scale its loan portfolio through cheaper access to the bond market. That's a good thing for margin growth. At MSDL, they're actively working to optimize their current borrowing terms. In fact, just recently, the company closed its CLO issue with a rate of SOFR + 1.70%.

I would now like to draw your attention to the following table, where a comparison of a number of key indicators and metrics is presented.

The main metric in assessing the appeal of BDC assets for me is the Total Return indicator. Let's take a look at its value over the past year. Both companies are unprofitable. They differ only in that BXSL's loss is 2.46% less. Take a look at the two-year period (with the only adjustment being that it starts on January 23, 2024, because BXSL starts on that day). Here, we see different results, because MSDL remains unprofitable, whereas BXSL has a profit of 10.14%. I still think this is risky, since the Fed is going to keep cutting rates. And that's going to hurt net income, making my recommendation for these assets "Sell."

MSDL and BXSL valuation

In order to assess the attractiveness of MSDL and BXSL, let's first look at the P/NAV metric. This is negative for both companies. The biggest drop is seen in MSDL, with its shares trading at a discount of 0.78x, whereas BXSL is trading at 0.94x. There are 3 main reasons why MSDL shares have such a huge discount, whereas BXSL is closer to its par value:

  • Since the net asset value of MSDL is declining, markets are assessing the additional risks associated with the impact of floating rates. Around 99.6% of all loans are issued at floating rates, meaning that the net asset value will continue to decline. Given that there are no reserves for dividend payments, in my opinion, shareholder payouts are likely to be reduced.
  • The MSDL is not a long-standing player in the BDC sector and has not yet established itself as a "blue chip." No situation has yet arisen that would allow the company's portfolio to be truly tested for stress resistance. Since the economic situation of BDC began to deteriorate in 2025, the focus of investors is on selling less reliable assets first.
  • The size of MSDL's portfolio is $3.8 billion, with a market capitalization of $1.4 billion, lagging behind the major players in BDC. This asset therefore has lower liquidity, meaning it attracts less investment from various institutional investors.

Risks associated with Fed rate cuts

Floating rate loans account for 99.6% of MSDL's portfolio and 98.6% of BXSL's portfolio. This high proportion of floating rates is particularly unfortunate given that the current cycle of Fed rate cuts is likely to continue. The next Fed rate cut is already forecast for the meeting on April 29, 2026. Currently, there is a 54.0% probability of a 25 basis point cut (to 3.50%). Each time the Fed cuts rates, SOFR will also fall. That increases interest rate risk for companies such as MSDL and BXSL. That's why the main risk for these assets is the ability to maintain their previous margins. Lower margins make it difficult to pay the same dividends as before. Therefore, a reduction in regular payments could increase investor concerns about MSDL and BXSL, as a result of which the further dynamics of their share prices will continue to move within the previous bearish trend. In 2025, a high dividend yield of around 12% per annum was only able to offset half of the loss in share value. This is why the overall return was negative (-13.28% and -10.82%).

Conclusion

In this way, both MSDL and BXSL are quite similar, placing them in the same category of BDC companies. Both are focused on minimizing capital loss risk and pay high dividends (around 12% per annum). However, they have one of the lowest base fees (1.0%), while access to parent company borrowers increases their scaling potential. It is only the key metrics, like P/NAV and Regular Dividend Coverage, show a big difference, so the market and the huge discount to shares think MSDL is one of the BDC "outsiders." As a conservative investor, you should probably go with BXSL. But personally, I’d recommend "Sell". If the Fed continues its policy of easing conditions in the money market, I don't believe this interest rate-sensitive asset class will be able to maintain its previous payouts. Instead, choose other BDC assets that are capable of gaining additional dividends from the new cycle of rate cuts (for example, Hercules Capital (HTGC) and Ares Capital (ARCC)).

data.title: Phathom: Why I'm Not Buying The Dip (Yet)
data.description:
Introduction

Despite some lingering reservations, I have become less cautious of Phathom Pharmaceuticals (PHAT) over time.

My last analysis in September 2025 followed the achievement of VOQUEZNA’s NCE exclusivity extension into 2032, which was a critical development for the company.

Moreover, Phathom has optimized its sales efforts, focusing less on costly and low ROI direct-to-consumer ads and more on prescriber outreach, driving operating leverage.

Still, while I acknowledged that VOQUEZNA plays a clear role in severe erosive GERD, Phathom’s balance sheet (which includes debt obligations, revenue liability principal, and so on) means that “$1 of VOQUEZNA net revenue ≠ $1 of cash.” Moreover, I asked, “Can the company justify its valuation with just six or seven years of VOQUEZNA? And at what expense will this come to its shareholders if they don’t achieve profitability in 2026 as guided?”

I concluded that PHAT was a “mixed bag” and assigned a Hold rating.

On Thursday, the company released preliminary Q4 results and announced its intention to raise $130 million via a public offering.

The article that follows revisits its stock in light of recent developments.

Recent Developments

To say it upfront, Phathom is still less “near-term IP cliff” and more “can it convert a finite window into durable cash flow despite leverage and GTN?”

Its preliminary Q4 results and public offering support this assertion.

Q4 net revenue is estimated as ~$57-$58 million. Non-GAAP operating expenses were ~$51-$53 million with ~$6 million net cash usage. The company ended 2025 with ~$130 million in cash.

Recall that Phathom originally guided operating profitability for 2026. This was tightened to H2 2026 (excluding stock comp).

The equity financing priced $130 million gross at $16.00 per share. While this reduces near-term solvency risk, it also reinforces that Phathom’s balance sheet remains a gating factor.

The market did not respond kindly to these developments. As I am typing these words, PHAT is off ~13% in intraday trading.

Reading between the lines, the Q4 result implies that VOQUEZNA’s revenue ramp is still steep. Q3 net revenue was $49.5 million, so the Q4 prerelease implies a sequential step-up of roughly $7.5-$8.5 million (~15-17% Q/Q). This does weaken the “Phathom cannot execute the launch fast enough” bear thesis.

Similarly, “1 million dispensed prescriptions” for VOQUEZNA products implies that ~210k additional dispensed scripts after 10/17/25. Recall that in July, gastroenterologists (the specialists who manage severe erosive disease) accounted for ~70% of filled VOQUEZNA scripts to date, which supports that growth is occurring in the segment where VOQUEZNA’s differentiation is most defensible (erosive disease/severe GERD), as opposed to broad first-line replacement of PPIs, where GTN is theoretically much lower.

Circling back to costs, the cost reset looks real on a run-rate basis. Recall that Q2 non-GAAP opex were $86.1 million. The Q4 prerelease implies $51-$53 million non-GAAP opex, which falls below its earlier restructuring target of sub-$55 million exiting 2025. So, the 2026 “profitability” debate is now less about SG&A bloat and more about GTN stability and fixed charges outside opex lines.

Finally, the equity raise, which will confer roughly 11% dilution, reflects the reality that “operating profitability” is not the same as being free cash flow positive once royalties, interest, and other obligations are accounted for. This helps balance Phathom’s outlook in the opposite direction in that it validates that VOQUEZNA’s commercial gains are muddied by deep and ongoing obligations.

Valuation

As for inputs, Year 1 Revenue of $316.41 million is the consensus analyst estimate for 2026. Revenue Growth and FCF Margin of 15% and 20%, respectively, are my base estimates. The FCF Margin is an “assumption within an assumption” because we are also assuming that Phathom will be able to generate cash (this is not yet the case). Remember, both values are averages.

Most everything else was gathered from Phathom’s financial statements (e.g., Interest-Bearing Debt, Cost of Debt estimate, Cash & Equivalents) or is standard DCF methodology (e.g., Tax Rate, Terminal Growth).

Two things catch my attention here. For starters, even when I plug in optimistic assumptions (15% CAGR and 20% FCF margin), PHAT is overvalued by ~26%. Second, as is typical in DCF models, PHAT’s valuation depends a lot on the terminal value (cash flows after Year 9). So, a key question that should sit atop investors' minds is, “What does Phathom look like after VOQUEZNA’s NCE exclusivity expires in 2032?” Note that 2032 coincides with Year 7 in my model. In pharma, terminal value is very dependent on R&D innovation, which requires R&D investments. However, due to ongoing losses and debt/RIFI obligations, the company is restricted there, and its pipeline is tied to just one drug with limited label expansion opportunities (e.g., as-needed dosing for “heartburn” and treatment of eosinophilic esophagitis).

Upon performing a Sensitivity Analysis, PHAT appears priced for 18.7% CAGR (assuming 20% FCF margin). Similarly, if FCF margin bumps to 24.6% (with CAGR remaining at 15%), PHAT becomes appropriately valued.

As you may already be aware, judging by PHAT’s 52-week range ($2.21-$18.31), it is ultra-sensitive to assumptions.

My Monte Carlo simulation shows how even small changes to key assumptions impact valuation.

For now, pending fundamental changes that would materially impact assumptions, PHAT’s “floor” and “ceiling” prices can be interpreted as $7.67 and $24.76. Of course, this is a wide range, consistent with the uncertainty involved with PHAT.

Final Words

Net-net, I remain at Hold.

While VOQUEZNA is a drug to be taken seriously, Phathom is tasked with driving volume and resetting costs. The Q4 prerelease signals that both are moving in the right direction.

On the other hand, the equity raise is a reminder that the balance sheet remains a gating factor. Operating profitability in H2 2026 is a nice goal to have, but don’t confuse it yet with free cash flow once interest, royalties, and the RIFI structure are accounted for.

On valuation, my base case requires continued execution and does not leave a lot of room for error. After all, PHAT is up >100% in the past year, so it would not surprise me to see it cool off here.

Until there is clearer evidence of durable GTN, a steadier path to free cash flow, reducing financing overhang, and better long-term prospects (that support terminal value assumptions), I’m happy to remain on the sidelines.

data.title: December U.S. Jobs Report Shows Weaker Hiring
data.description:
By Krzysztof Kamiński

The December US employment report confirmed a clear slowdown in hiring that had been visible throughout the past year. Data published by the Bureau of Labor Statistics show that nonfarm payrolls increased by just 50,000 in December, well below market expectations of 70,000. At the same time, the unemployment rate edged down slightly to 4.4%.

While the decline in unemployment may suggest stability in the labor market, the details of the report point to a more complex picture.

Uneven Hiring Across Sectors

Job growth was concentrated mainly in leisure and hospitality as well as health care. These industries accounted for the majority of new jobs both in December and throughout 2025. At the same time, five of the eleven major sectors of the economy recorded declines in employment, including retail trade, construction, and manufacturing.

Private-sector employers added only 37,000 jobs, a fraction of the gains seen in the same period a year earlier.

Data Revisions and a Weak Annual Balance

In addition, payroll figures for October and November were revised down by a combined 76,000 jobs, further weakening the picture of labor market momentum. For the full year 2025, employment rose by just 584,000, making it the weakest year for job creation since 2020, when the Covid-19 pandemic triggered a sharp collapse in the labor market.

Labor Force Participation and Long-Term Unemployment

The labor force participation rate slipped to 62.4%, while the share of workers aged 25 to 54 - known as prime-age workers - remained steady. Meanwhile, the number of long-term unemployed people, defined as those out of work for 27 weeks or more, rose by nearly 400,000 in 2025, the largest annual increase since the pandemic.

The number of people working part-time for economic reasons also increased sharply, highlighting growing uncertainty among workers.

Market Reaction and the Federal Reserve’s Stance

As the labor market gradually cooled, the Federal Reserve cut interest rates three times toward the end of 2025. Following the release of the December report, however, investors began unwinding bets on further rate cuts. Treasury yields rose, and markets now expect the Fed to keep rates unchanged at its January meeting.

According to Bloomberg economists, the latest jobs data may have raised concerns among policymakers, but not enough to justify a swift return to rate cuts.

Wages and Consumer Sentiment

Average hourly earnings rose by 0.3% month over month in December, in line with expectations. Although wage growth has been slowing, it remains a key driver of consumer spending, which has become increasingly concentrated among wealthier households.

Despite some recent signs of improvement, consumer confidence remains subdued and close to record lows.

Cautious Outlook for the Year Ahead

Economists expect the labor market to remain weak in the coming months, with limited job opportunities and further cooling in wage growth. This outlook could intensify household concerns about affordability and financial security, particularly ahead of upcoming congressional elections. As a result, the US labor market enters the new year in a fragile balance — without a sharp rise in unemployment, but also without clear momentum for a rebound.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Employment Report: 50K Jobs Added In December, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The latest employment report showed that 50,000 jobs were added in December, down slightly from November's 56,000 addition. This figure was lower than the forecast of 66,000. Meanwhile, the unemployment rate fell to 4.4%, coming in lower than the projected 4.5% rate.

Here is an excerpt from the Employment Situation Summary released this morning by the Bureau of Labor Statistics:

Both total nonfarm payroll employment (+50,000) and the unemployment rate (4.4 percent) changed little in December, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Employment continued to trend up in food services and drinking places, health care, and social assistance. Retail trade lost jobs.

Household Survey Data: Both the unemployment rate, at 4.4 percent, and the number of unemployed people, at 7.5 million, changed little in December.

Establishment Survey Data: Total nonfarm payroll employment changed little in December (+50,000). Employment continued to trend up in food services and drinking places, health care, and social assistance. Retail trade lost jobs. Payroll employment rose by 584,000 in 2025 (an average monthly gain of 49,000), less than the increase of 2.0 million in 2024 (an average monthly gain of 168,000).

Here is a snapshot of the monthly change in nonfarm employment over the last five years. The 3-month moving average is currently at -22,000.

For another view, here is the monthly percent change in nonfarm employment since 2000. We've added a 12-month moving average to highlight the long-term trend. The latest 12-month moving average is at 49,000, its lowest level since the initial aftermath of the pandemic.

Unemployment, Recessions, and Market Trends

The next chart illustrates the relationship between unemployment, recessions, and the S&P Composite since 1948. Unemployment is typically a lagging indicator that moves inversely to equity prices (the top series in the chart).

Notice the rising unemployment peaks in 1971, 1975, and 1982, which coincided with bear markets. A similar pattern briefly emerged during the COVID pandemic, but the impact was short-lived as irrational exuberance quickly took over. The latest unemployment rate stands at 4.375% (to three decimal places).

Now, let's examine the unemployment rate as a recession indicator - specifically, the cyclical troughs in the unemployment rate (UR). The next chart highlights a 12-month moving average of the UR, with its troughs marked.

Currently, the unemployment rate stands at 4.38%, higher than the latest 12-month moving average of 4.25%. As shown in the inset table, the correlation between these moving average troughs and the start of recessions is remarkably strong. The most recent trough occurred 30 months ago, in June 2023, when the 12-month moving average of the unemployment rate fell to its lowest level since January 1970.

The next chart highlights the unemployment rate for those unemployed for 27 weeks or more. This rate has declined significantly from its all-time peak of 4.4% in April 2010. Following the COVID pandemic, it rose as high as 2.6% but quickly fell. This rate has recently been trending upwards and now stands at 1.1%.

How long does unemployment typically last? As the next chart shows, the latest data indicates that the average duration of unemployment is 24.4 weeks. Historically, this metric tends to rise during and after recessions.

The Bureau of Labor Statistics’ broadest measure of unemployment, the U6 series, captures not only the unemployed but also the underemployed, marginally attached workers, and those who have stopped looking for work. Often referred to as the 'real' unemployment rate, many economists consider it the most comprehensive gauge of labor market conditions.

In contrast, the more widely cited unemployment rate only includes those who are unemployed and actively job-seeking within the past four weeks. The U6 series currently stands at 8.4%, down from 8.7% in November.

Labor Market Participation and Structural Shifts

For a broader perspective on the structural shifts in the U.S. labor market, the next chart illustrates the labor force participation rate. This metric measures the percentage of the civilian population aged 16 and over who are either employed or actively seeking work.

It provides a key estimate of an economy’s active workforce and serves as an important indicator of labor market health. Currently, the rate stands at 62.4%, down slightly from November.

Here’s another chart that provides a different perspective on the unemployment rate - unemployment divided by the labor force participation rate. This metric currently stands at 7.0%, down from 7.3% in November.

The next chart overlays the unemployment rate with the employment-population ratio, which measures the number of employed people as a percentage of the total civilian population aged 16 and over. The inverse correlation between the two series is clear.

The data also highlights the accelerating growth of women in the workforce and the rise of two-income households in the early 1980s. Before the COVID pandemic, the employment-population ratio fluctuated between 54.9% and 64.7%.

The lower end of this range dates back to the Eisenhower era - a time of predominantly one-income households, the Korean War, and the novelty of soft drinks being sold in cans.

In April 2020, the global pandemic drove the employment-population ratio to a record low of 51.2%. Since then, it has been gradually recovering toward its pre-pandemic range, with the latest reading at 59.7%, up slightly from November.

Looking ahead, both the employment-population ratio and labor force participation rate will be key metrics to watch. The first wave of baby boomers continues to exert downward pressure on these figures. The oldest boomers became eligible for early retirement at the onset of the Great Recession, and their transition to full retirement age will continue through 2030.

Notes: The start date of 1948 in the charts above was determined by the earliest monthly employment data collected by the Bureau of Labor Statistics. The best source for the historical data is the Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis.

The S&P Composite is a splice of the S&P 500, which started in 1957, with the S&P 90, which preceded it. ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), and SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPYM).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Reddit: Early Facebook, But Without The Negativity, I'm In
data.description:
Introduction

As an avid lurker on Reddit, Inc. (RDDT) and its many subreddits, it is surprising that I never got around to looking at the company in more detail until now. The company has had an amazing run since going public, with no signs of stopping, and I can’t help but draw parallels to another social media company that had an amazing run in the past, but without the negativity surrounding it for now. I am giving Reddit a Buy rating.

Performance

Since I am covering the company for the first time, it is important to check RDDT’s sales performance over its lifetime, and that is not a long life. From the end of 2020, the days of lockdowns and everyone glued to their screens, RDDT’s sales almost 10x’ed, going from around $228m to $1.9B. The latest quarter showed us that the company’s revenues for the nine months ended September 30th stood at around $1.5B, with an additional $660m to be made in the last quarter of ’25, so over $2.1B for the year. Since going public back in March of ’24, the growth remained strong.

Looking at the company’s profitability, we can see that the company is quite profitable already, but seeing that its gross margins are over 91%, its operating margins, and the rest of the margins are quite low. In Q1’24, the company massively increased its R&D spending, and it’s a good thing that RDDT’s top line grew substantially since then, which helped outgrow the expenses and made it profitable once again. The over 91% gross margin allows for a lot of leverage and scalability in the future, as evidenced by a whopping 68% growth in sales, while expenses grew by around 31%. If the company continues this trend, margins across the board will grow significantly.

Taking a look at other important metrics to me, specifically the ROE, ROA, and ROTC, we can see that these are rising just as the company’s bottom line is, which are already at decent returns, especially ROA and ROE. ROTC, I believe, will easily climb over 10% over the next few quarters. Reddit has become a lot more profitable in a very short time, and that is due to its massive, engaged customer base. I believe this trend is going to continue.

Looking at Reddit’s financial position as of the latest quarter, we can see it is flush with cash. $2.2B in liquidity available in the form of cash and marketable securities against essentially no debt besides some operating lease liabilities, which are negligible. That is a very strong balance sheet, allowing the company to focus its efforts 100% towards growing the platform, researching ways to monetize the massive, engaged audience, and improving the experience overall.

The growth has been astounding. The social media business model is so lucrative that it doesn’t take many years to be profitable, let alone break even. With such numbers that the company is putting out quarter after quarter, I can see the multiples shrinking considerably over the next few years.

Advertising- The most Lucrative business model

As with all social media companies, or at least the top ones like Alphabet (GOOG) and Meta Platforms (META), RDDT’s business model comprises over 90% of revenues made through advertising. Ad revenue increased 74% this past quarter, making over $549m of total revenues, so it is safe to say that right now, RDDT is an ads business, and there is nothing wrong with that. Such growth in sales wouldn’t be possible without a very dedicated user base. In the latest quarter, daily active unique, or DAUq, continues to grow at a decent pace, around 19%. The international DAUq grew at a respectable 31% compared to the US 7% growth y/y. The Global Weekly average unique numbers grew by 21%, with international leading the way with 37% y/y growth. These numbers are fantastic, which led to average revenue per user, or ARPU, growing substantially across all regions. ARPU in the US grew 54% y/y, international grew 39%, bringing down total ARPU to around 41% y/y. US ARPU leads by a massive difference compared to the international ARPU numbers, as you can see below.

It seems that advertisers are starting to like RDDT’s advertising platform for their products, which is evident in over 70% growth in ad revenue. Clearly outpacing the juggernauts of ad revenue like META and GOOG, which saw 26% growth and 12.7%, respectively. However, the low base of RDDT helps it shine here, since other companies are already mature with billions of daily active users under their belts. Nevertheless, advertisers are beginning to see the advantage of RDDT communities. You may be asking why?

There are a couple of reasons why RDDT is becoming the choice of so many advertisers. Probably one of the main ones is that the average cost per mille, or CPM, is much lower than what META and GOOG charge. RDDT uses an auction model, so CPM can range from about $1 to $6 for broader placements, which on average turns out to be around $3.5. Compare this to META’s CPM on average for Facebook was $14.40, and for Instagram, $13.20. Google CPM ranges from $4-$9, which is a lot better than META. So, as costs go, RDDT takes the cake; however, you can say that the reason it is low is because of the reach. Meta and Google have billions of clicks daily, so their reach is superior compared to RDDT, which means most likely in dollar terms, a big company is better off paying for ads on these two platforms and not RDDT. It is also worth looking at the efficiency of these ads. Having such al ow entry point to advertise on RDDT, the return on ad spend, or ROAS, can often be a lot more attractive than META or GOOG.

The conversion rates are quite a bit higher compared to Facebook and Microsoft's (MSFT) LinkedIn, with RDDT’s rates ranging anywhere from 3%-6%, while Facebook and LinkedIn at 2%-4% and 1%-3%, respectively. The advantage of Reddit’s niche markets and more focused communities, which may be a lot more willing to spend money, is a lot more attractive for advertisers as they get a much better bang for their buck. RDDT’s niche users skew towards higher-income, with 26% of the users earning more than $75k. These people engage in communities like r/personalfinance, which advertisers love to put their ads on due to the nature of the subreddit. Finance pays. If ARPU going from $5.88 to $9 in a year is not proof enough that these users are willing to spend, then I don’t know what is.

I believe advertisers will continue to flock to the platform going forward, as it is going to be hard to beat such numbers. When you can reach fewer people but more efficiently convert them, means you are either doing as well as on the juggernaut platforms or better, since you spent a lot less. The company is constantly improving ads platform using AI to see where it is best to put an ad that will grab the most eyes. In the most recent conference calls, the executives said that AI/ML optimizations helped boost lower-funnel performance by 20%.

Back in Early October of last year, the company’s shares slid for two days straight because, in September, ChatGPT data showed that RDDT’s content dropped to just 2% from around 10% earlier, sparking a massive panic selling. That was a bad couple of days for investors; however, recent articles have cited Reddit’s content as the top in AI search in over 20%, up to as much as 40%. So, there was no need for panic. The company’s shares promptly recovered.

Parallels to Facebook

I can’t help but draw comparisons to the early Facebook days. Both leverage hyper-engaged communities to target ads and are doing it rather successfully. Right now, it seems that Reddit is trading at a massive forward P/E ratio of 109. That is expensive; however, there were times when Facebook’s P/E ratio was astronomical, too. When it IPO’ed, its P/E ratio was over 1300, as its EPS was essentially nonexistent, at around 2 cents. The following year, it dropped to 88x as its EPS went to 62 cents, and then it continued to fall, at one point during the massive scandals I remember going as low as 10x when its business continued to be a massive cash cow.

I think we are seeing the same here with Reddit. Sure, a 100x P/E ratio is expensive, no doubt about it, but when you look at it from a long-term perspective, Reddit is still in its growth phase. As revenues from ads continue to grow, with minimal operating expenses to boot, the multiples will contract so quickly that these triple-digit ratios will be forgotten in an instant. Analysts are already projecting FY26 FW P/E ratio to be around 66, which will continue to fall a lot more over the following years.

What Reddit has going for it right now that Facebook doesn’t anymore is the public’s perception of the platform. Reddit right now enjoys a lot more positive sentiment than Meta Platforms. The regulatory scrutiny, privacy scandals, and advertiser boycotts that plagued Meta for many years and continue to plague it to this day are very different from what Reddit experiences. Anywhere you look, many people say the world would be better off without Meta. The doom-scrolling on its platforms is not helping anyone, while Reddit fuels conversation so people learn from different perspectives, which is a lot more productive than the AI slop I see appearing more and more on Instagram, which is one of the main reasons I haven’t been on it in a long time. Reddit, on the other hand, I am part of the Logged in DAUq metric.

Meta is a cash cow, and will most likely remain one, and I still wouldn’t hesitate to invest in it if it drops substantially on another privacy scandal. Reddit’s just getting started.

Valuation

So, it is hard to model a company that is still in its what I would call a hyper growth phase, but let’s look at some quick projections for the next 5 years. Below you’ll see the assumptions I used.

As you can see, these are rather optimistic. Revenue growth is very high, but I don’t think it is unreasonable given how its platform has been growing recently. Operating margins, however, are all speculation because right now, it is at around 22%, I believe, but since it is in that growth phase where margins will massively improve over time, I decided to go with what is achievable for Reddit. How do I know this is achievable? I looked at Meta’s operating margins.

In the most recent reports, GAAP operating margins were around 40% on around 82% gross margins. These margins are also down from the highs of around 48% because Meta is spending billions on AI capex. So, it isn’t unreasonable to think that, given RDDT’s 90%+ gross margins, it cannot reach at least 40% over the long term. The company’s adjusted EBITDA margin was already at 40.3%. The rest I am fine with going with its historical averages, as well as its WACC of 8.5% is decent. With these assumptions, we get the following EPS projections.

And with these projections, we get an intrinsic value of around $275 a share

Risks

The assumptions on profitability may take a hit if, for some reason, growth decides to slow down significantly.

Competition for advertisers could make them leave Reddit for something that is even better at converting clicks to paid customers, leading to a slowdown in the top-line growth and a loss of operating leverage.

The company currently has quite a high short interest of around 14%, which can bring a lot of volatility, and if the company reports anything but perfection, double-digit drops in share price are not out of the question.

The platform could see some regulatory scrutiny eventually, as did Meta, which will most likely make the shares a lot cheaper.

Users could become disgruntled with the way the platform works, and moderators begin to strike if there’s a change in the algorithm that alienates users.

AI citations once again could be a major threat in the future, and since the company is dependent on advertising, another advertiser winter can impact its operations considerably.

Investor Takeaway- High Risk, High Reward

I believe the platform is going to be around for many years to come. Now that it is publicly traded, I can see the growth continuing for a much longer time. This is one of those picks that I consider high risk, high reward. I will not be going all in, but will be opening a starter position as I believe multiples will contract significantly over the next couple of years, and the company will not look this expensive. I even think such a high P/E ratio is still justified given how much growth the company has had over the last few years.

Eventually, the 90%+ gross margins will translate into much higher operating profits and bottom line. So, I am giving Reddit a Buy rating. I think it is worth the risk.

data.title: Rogers Corporation: The Thesis For 2026 Is Mixed
data.description:
Since my first coverage of Rogers Corporation (ROG), following a failed merger, the company has provided mixed results. I managed to eke out an excellent gain in my first article, sold it off, and have enjoyed staying out since. It seems that since 2025 (for the entire year), I am the only one covering Rogers Corporation on SA. Unfortunately, I missed the bottom for company valuation, which came early to mid-2025, and the company has seen good performance since that article, rather than neutral/HOLD-like performance as my article implied. I underestimated the company's reversal potential despite what is without a doubt a very rich earnings multiple - over 35-40x P/E, no matter how you view this company. Even if the short-term effects of the ratings I gave ROG have been correct, even avoiding the deep short-term drawdown in March of last year, I haven't been on point with the medium-term RoR potential with the company.

Given that it's now over 6 months since my update on ROG, I thought it would be a good time for a "fresh" view on the business for the coming fiscal.

Rogers Corporation is a tricky business to understand if you don't actually do some deeper research into its end markets. On the surface, it's an engineered materials company. An engineered material is a substance with intentionality behind its design, manipulated to perform specific functions and outperform natural materials (usually by definition). Engineered materials are common in every sector of our world these days, and investing in them is usually put into the "chemical" industries, which is one of my early focal areas. Typical areas for the use of engineered materials are end-markets concerned with extreme specifics or performance, such as aerospace, electronics, and medicine.

That is also where we find Rogers Corporation.

Let's see what the company's prospects are.

Rogers Corporation - Expectations for 2026E

Rogers Corporation has several advantages, and even now, several years after the fact, I am surprised that the potential buyer let the company slip through its fingers. Its advantages are clear - it's a market leader or among market leadership in several crucial fields that seem to be a key component of the modern digital world. This includes high-performance advanced connectivity applications for 5G and Wireless, including EVs, as well as owning PORON foams and silicones for sealing and cushioning, critical applications across several high-demand sectors such as aerospace and automotive.

Its international profile allows it some insulation, if you pardon the pun, for protectionism and tariffs, thanks to variability in production and overall flexibility, with presence including Europe, Asia (outside of China as well, in South Korea and Japan). I personally believe the commoditized chemicals sector is bound to see trouble and issues going forward, while more specialized sectors are bound to see better overall trends. Because of the company's brands, including such as RT/duroid®, Kappa®, BISCO®, PORON®, and XRD®, this company has carved a compelling little niche for itself and its roughly $1.7B of market capitalization.

The company's performance since 2023 has been poor; however, so exiting at the right time, if you look at what has happened since, was definitely the right choice. The key reason for the company's lack of solid performance was not operational issues or poor execution, but a lack of demand increase from the end-markets, coupled with what I view as a significant overvaluation in a market where many high-quality chemical players are trading at sub-10x P/E.

The broader issues and challenges in the global economy have also contributed to a relatively negative performance, and the company's margins have suffered as a result of this.

The latest results we have at this time are the 3Q25 results, and these were above my own expectations and targets, hitting the high end of the company's own expectations.

The growth challenges overall that the market is seeing are reflected in the company's Quant grading, where growth has a challenged "D" rating. Wall Street averages disagree with the Quant of "HOLD", giving the company a "STRONG BUY" rating, which in my view reflects a perhaps too-bullish view of the reversal potential in key end markets. Even if some of the company's end markets see improvement, I wouldn't view the company's appeal as that high here.

The positives include, for 3Q25, a solid EBITDA margin and top-line increases.

The name of the game for the company is capacity adjustments to demand, not over-utilizing existing assets, and being careful with capital allocation. Rogers is also reducing its footprint where necessary, seeing savings and operating expense cuts. Recent examples include the restructuring of a German asset, as well as being very selective with growth options and potential.

Most of the company's end markets are what I would consider "tricky". Industrial is probably the most stable, ironically, which also saw sales increases in 3Q25, but industrial is generally not known for being "involatile". But compared to end markets like ADAS, EV, A&D, and renewables, it can certainly be argued to be more stable than those.

Operationally speaking, Rogers is doing the right things at the right time. EBITDA is solid and growing.

The margin increase you see above is very impressive, and it's based on a very strict usage of cash. The company speaks about returning capital to shareholders, in this context $10M worth of share buybacks, but I still don't consider this to be a prudent use of cash here. I believe more should be pushed towards restructuring and improving assets, as I don't believe the company is clearly attractive enough from a valuation perspective to justify these buybacks.

The outlook for the company's 2026E is mixed. 2025E is expected to be a "bottom" year, with an AEPS decline of over 22% compared to 2024. After that, an expectation of $3/share in AEPS is set for 2026E, which would be a 42% YoY increase, followed by yet another 40%+ improvement in 2027E.

The thing about these forecasts is that they are so based and dependent on cyclical recovery in uncertain markets that, in my view, are not forecastable enough where one can confidently make a 2-year forecast, that it shouldn't be used as a base for a 25-40x P/E forecast. As an example, less than 1 month ago, the analyst forecast for 2026E was $2.6, which would be less than a 30% growth - now it's 42%. 6 months ago, it was $3.2/share, a 51% increase, with no forecasts set for 2027E.

I show this to illustrate how uncertain and volatile these trends are, and what can be expected here.

Valuation for Rogers is currently as follows.

Rogers Corporation - Upside is uncertain, and over 40% forecast uncertainty doesn't make it easier.

The upside for Rogers here, provided the forecasts are accurate and that we can depend on a 5-year average P/E multiple, is market-beating at 21% per year, annualized. But due to what I showed you above, and a historical tendency towards not hitting the stated or forecasted targets, coupled with a definite premium to alternative chemical producers, as well as no dividend, leads me to discount and be somewhat more dubious on the company here.

Several things speak in Rogers' favor. The low debt (less than 3% long-term debt to capital) and the company's portfolio. Unfortunately, I do not believe this to be enough for an attractive investment for several reasons.

Most important is that the market as a whole is overvalued here. Expecting outperformance in this market, which in my view has a real chance to see a significant correction, is dangerous. Secondly, the inflated P/E premium relative to other companies in the same field is far too high. Littlefuse (LFUS) trades at less than 26x P/E, has a yield with a near-15-year tradition, does not miss targets as often (beats them), and is almost 5x the size of Rogers. Even at a 20-year P/E discount to 23.6x, LFUS is more attractive from a straight valuation perspective at this time.

Given that the upside is less than 15% annualized, I do not consider LFUS a "BUY" - I am only saying it's more attractive relative to ROG. Despite size and forecast security, I would not consider the giant Amphenol (APH) to be a "BUY" here. Trading at over 40x P/E but with an upside that provides only market-beating return in case of a very solid overall trend in 2028E, I do not consider this safe enough to invest in either, despite an A-rating and a $166B+ market cap.

I would consider this time a good time to stay away from investments in this sector until things calm down a bit. If you want chemical exposure at this time, I would move from engineered materials to either commodity distribution/intermediaries like Brenntag (BNTGF), or specialized chemical manufacturers trading at dirt-cheap valuations such as Evonik (EVKIY).

At this time, I would give Rogers no more than a 25x forward P/E, coming to about $55/share in a PT, and a fair value of roughly $75/share for the 2026E, based on a 30-40% EPS growth.

Rogers and its peers may, and likely will, become more attractive in the future, but not now.

Risks to the investment are currently as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The single highest risk to Rogers Corporation is, in my view, the excessive valuation of the company coupled with its fundamentals. By fundamentals, I am referring to the fact that the company isn't the safest around, and it lacks a dividend. This is generally atypical of a chemical company, which tends to have attractive dividends, or at the very least some sort of interest to lean on.

Aside from fundamentals, risks are related to macro-things like China, things like industrial fluctuations and trade/tariffs, and protectionism (on the rise), as well as overall delays in technologies which the company works to enable, such as Electric Vehicles (EV).

Such things have the potential to make the company's competitors more attractive from a local perspective, competitors like Littlefuse, Amphenol, Materon, Avient, and even highly specialized companies such as ATI or Quanex.

If there's one thing I've learned from investing in Chemicals for over 10 years, it's that very few companies have products that are not replaceable by other businesses. The best protection that companies in this sector have is, in my view, scale and patent protection, as well as long-lasting consumer relationships.

Based on this, I view the company as attractive at the right price and with the right upside - but today, the volatility in overall markets is high.

Thesis

This was an undervalued play following a failed merger with DuPont several years ago, in specialty materials and engineered components/substrates for various attractive end uses and industries.

This sort of company typically deserves a premium, and if the company had size, a credit rating, and yield, it would have deserved more, such as my expectations for a peer like Amphenol.

For now, in 2026, however, Rogers is valued at a too high level compared to what else is available and its lack of a dividend. The macro also plays a major role in my considerations here.

I am upgrading my PT for entry to about $55/share again, reflecting my previous, 2024 PT (close to), but the company has a long way to drop to be attractive here.

I work with a rating of "HOLD" here. I don't own any shares in the company at this time, nor do I intend to

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 2 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 2/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a price target of $55/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

data.title: Coincheck: M&A Moves Address Prior Concerns
data.description:
About a year ago, I initiated coverage of Coincheck Group N.V. (CNCK) for Seeking Alpha. The company primarily operates a Japan-based cryptocurrency exchange. Back then, I called the stock a Hold and cited what I felt where way too many risks to consider longing the stock:

There is valuation risk, single jurisdiction risk, and market risk. Demand for crypto assets in Japan simply has not been as robust as it is in the rest of the world.

In hindsight, a Sell call would have been more appropriate as the stock has fallen by over 70% since my article last January. Given the massive slide in the stock's price as well as several catalysts since the last note, it's time to revisit CNCK.

M&A Activity

Over the last year, Coincheck has been quite active with mergers and acquisitions. In October, the company completed its purchase of crypto prime brokerage Aplo SAS in an all-stock transaction. Earlier in 2025, Coincheck bought Next Finance Tech Ltd in an effort to eliminate the need for the third party staking services the company had previously been using as part of its Coincheck Staking product.

More recently, the company agreed to acquire Canadian digital asset manager 3iQ from Monex Group - the latter of which is a major shareholder of CNCK stock. The deal, which will again be an all-stock transaction, would result in 27,149,684 new shares being issued. While the dilution is significant given 130.8 million shares outstanding at the end of September and likely 5-6 million shares for the Aplo deal in October, these mergers do address one of the primary concerns that I had last year when I first covered the stock. Specifically, that crypto-interest levels from the Japanese market was lackluster and a company operating a retail investor crypto exchange solely in Japan was a potentially dubious investment.

With the Aplo deal, Coincheck adds an institutional investor footprint in Europe. And assuming the 3iQ deal closes, Coincheck will have ETF assets managed in the Canadian market as well. Based on my calculations of 3iQ's management fees relative to the $1.1 billion in AUM currently managed by the company, Coincheck is paying a little under 7x sales to buy 3iQ. This puts the valuation of 3iQ in line with BlackRock (BLK). Furthermore, Monex Group did the deal at $4 per share while CNCK stock trades under $3. My interpretation of this is Monex is simply consolidating its crypto business assets while also signaling to the market that it is committed to holding the newly issued shares since the market values them well below $4.

Coincheck Metrics

One of the metrics that Coincheck frequently shares to show growth is its verified accounts for the Coincheck platform. As of the end of December, that figure was just under 2.5 million. Something to consider is that month over month growth in verified accounts did go down slightly during the three months ended December. During calendar Q4 '24, monthly verified account growth averaged 1.5% each month. That figure was down to just 0.7% during calendar Q4-25.

For the benefit of simplicity, we're going to also look at the quarterly exchange data through calendar year quarters rather than fiscal year quarters. While we don't yet have full financials for Coincheck through the end of calendar 2025, we do have the exchange volume data through the end of the year and it isn't really a pretty story.

Calendar Quarters Exchange Volume (millions Yen) YoY Change
Q2-24 1,050,308
Q3-24 1,062,632
Q4-24 1,674,094
Q1-25 1,459,506
Q2-25 1,052,175 0.18%
Q3-25 949,466 -10.65%
Q4-25 1,209,344 -27.76%

Source: Coincheck filings.

The table above shows that year over year exchange volume in 2025 has declined in each of the last two quarters. To be sure, Q4 '25 has a difficult year over year comparison due to the strength of Q4 '24. But even if we take the average exchange volume from the last three quarters compared to the prior year, 2025 was down by 15% from the 2024 time period.

Latest Financials

Despite the weakness in exchange volume during the 3 months ended September, Coincheck was able to generate a 58% increase in dollar-denominated top line revenue from the prior quarter and an 84% increase year over year. In Q2 for fiscal 2026, Coincheck reported $900 million in total revenue and a net profit of $2.4 million:

This was a sequential improvement from FY26-Q1 where Coincheck reported a $9.3 million net loss. The company said the biggest reason for the improvement in the bottom line was gross margin growth as well as declines in SG&A during the quarter. Given the cash burn through the first two quarters of fiscal year 2026, Coincheck has several quarters of cash remaining before it would figure to need to raise capital with over $60 million in cash and equivalents:

A large amount of the assets held by the company are customer assets and borrowed crypto assets. Netting out those deposits and crypto borrowings, Coincheck has a little over $70 million in equity. Which means that at a $368 million market capitalization, CNCK is trading at a little over 5 times book value. Which is a bit pricey for the financials sector median of 1.3 and a little higher than the 4x book multiple the market is currently giving Coinbase (COIN). And again, there is likely going to be another 27 million shares issued when the company closes the 3iQ deal.

Risks

There are still several risks that potential investors should consider before longing CNCK. Given the combination of growing users and declining volume in 2025 through the company's legacy product, I think it's pretty clear Coincheck had to do something to stimulate top line revenue growth going forward. M&A is certainly a way to do that provided it brings complementary pieces into the company. In my view, Coincheck is improving its portfolio of assets out of a single jurisdiction and into synergistic businesses. But it is coming at a cost to shareholders through dilution. This is something I think investors need to continue to monitor due to Coincheck's stated goals from its prior earnings presentation. The company apparently wants to get into payments and lending as well.

Closing Summary

Believe it or not, I don't actually hate a speculative CNCK long under $3 per share. I'm not personally holding the stock currently. But in a broader risk market rally that takes stocks and crypto higher, I think CNCK could potentially see gains. Shares traded as high as $9 less than two months ago.

There was a massive intraday move on December 1st that saw over 27 million shares traded during a rally that more than doubled the stock price. Since then, CNCK shares have bled down to a fresh 52 week low before rallying back up over $3. The point is, it's an incredibly volatile stock right now. And if we see digital assets make a move higher, it would figure to bring CNCK shares higher in tandem. To be sure, this is not necessarily a great reason to "invest" in the stock. But if you're looking for high-risk, high-reward plays in the digital asset space, CNCK may not be a bad option.

data.title: Hubbell Strongly Leveraged To Electrification Growth, But Also Priced Accordingly
data.description:
Hubbell Incorporated's (HUBB) performance since my last update on this supplier of electrical and utility components has been interesting. Utility demand has been positive overall (outside of metering), and data center demand has been strong, but overall financial performance has tracked slightly below my expectations. Even so, the company remains well-placed to leverage secular growth trends like “electrify everything”, reshoring, utility upgrade cycles, and data center capex.

Hubbell shares are up a little less than 50% since that last article, basically tracking in line with the larger industrial sector and likewise matching the performance of other electrification plays like Rexel (RXEEY) (RXLSF) and Schneider (SBGSY), while slightly underperforming Eaton (ETN) and more significantly underperforming nVent (NVT).

I do think that Hubbell is a differentiated growth story in the industrial space with attractive growth and margin leverage opportunities over the next five-plus years. I also think, though, that the market largely appreciates it as that, and it’s hard for me to argue that the shares are significantly undervalued.

Reacceleration In Q4, But Can They Finally Meet (Let Alone Beat) Expectations?

One of the issues that pops up in looking at Hubbell’s recent performance is the string of top-line misses that stretches back to the second quarter of 2022. These haven’t been big misses (often around 2%) and you could perhaps blame sell-side analysts for errors in modeling, but the reality is that Hubbell management themselves have lowered guidance along the way as well.

This creates an interesting setup for the upcoming fourth quarter report. On one hand, management is guiding for a significant rebound in organic growth – from around 3% in Q3’25 to 8% to 10% in Q4 and a level of growth that should have the company in the top ranks of organic growth among the industrials I follow. On the other hand, can they actually come in at or above expectations and carry some sentiment momentum into 2026?

On the positive side, utility spending remains healthy, with distribution now joining transmission and substation as areas of growth/strength in the utility market. Data center spending also remains robust, with most companies with exposure to this vertical (electrical components or otherwise) seeing better than expected results. I also think that one of the main drags in recent quarters – metering and AMI – will be less of a drag; not because the business is substantially stronger, but because the year-ago comp is quite a bit easier (Grid Automation revenue was down 11% in last year’s fourth quarter, with metering/AMI pulling down growth in grid protection/controls).

I’m guardedly bullish that Hubbell will deliver growth in the 9%-9.5% range for the quarter and set the stage for a healthy 5% to 6% revenue growth guide for FY’26 (likely a range of 4% to 6%). I’m less concerned about margins, as Hubbell has done comparatively better here, and I think the company will see more than a point of adjusted operating margin leverage (to around 23%).

Drivers In Place For Above-Average Long-Term Growth

While most industrial companies target 3% to 4% long-term core revenue growth, the reality is that many end up more in the 2%-3% range. I expect Hubbell to be different, as the company is well-positioned to leverage above-average multiyear growth in electrical and utility spending.

Utility grid spending seems unlikely to slow meaningfully in the foreseeable future. A meaningful amount of spending (over a third of distribution spending and closer to a quarter of transmission spending) is going towards grid hardening, but there is still ongoing need for expansion spending to accommodate renewables generation and to address the increasing demands of increasingly electrified commercial buildings and factories, not to mention electricity-hungry data centers.

Above and beyond that, utilities continue to invest in grid protection, monitoring, and controls to not only protect assets from malicious attacks, but also disruptions from natural hazards. Moreover, automation not only improves customer satisfaction (avoiding or mitigating power disruptions, or at least fixing them faster), but also reduces operating costs, freeing up resources to reinvest in hardware needs.

Hubbell is also well-placed to benefit from ongoing manufacturing reshoring and electrification. Although reshoring has been much more significant in industries that already needed to add capacity (like semiconductors and communications equipment), it is still happening, and guidance from major automation providers like Rockwell (ROK) points to more growth on the way.

These new factories tend to be much more automated and electrified than in the past, driving demand for both Hubbell’s industrial products (like power quality systems, wiring systems, controls, et al.) and utility systems, as new factories often require utility-level components like substation switches. Additionally, companies continue to invest in electrification retrofits, whether to replace outdated and inefficient generation systems or to facilitate more automation within the facility.

Last and not least is data center capex growth. I don’t believe management has ever quantified the company’s exposure to this market, but it’s enough to move the needle for the Electrical Systems segment, and the company offers a range of products like cabinets, grounding systems, enclosures, cable management systems, and integrated units. With Eaton recently talking about a six to nine-year pipeline for DC projects at a sell-side conference, this can be a meaningful ongoing contributor to growth.

The Outlook

I’m relatively confident that utility spending will be healthy in 2026 (outside of metering), and I’m likewise confident in the ongoing growth in data center capex. I’m not as confident about the outlook for industrial and non-resi end-markets, though I think both should improve as the year progresses. I’m expecting over 6% growth next year, over 6% growth over the next three years, and long-term annualized growth of a little over 5%.

On the margins, I’ve been impressed with how management has opportunistically leveraged cost inflation to its long-term benefit. In the past, management has raised prices in response to cost input inflation (typically metals), but then held those prices even when costs abated. That has helped drive EBITDA margins from the mid-teens to the low/mid-20%’s, and I think input cost inflation and tariffs in 2025 could well set the stage for a repeat in the years to come (though not at quite the same magnitude).

I’m expecting adjusted EBITDA margin to exceed 25% in FY’26 and improve to 27%-28% in the years to come, helping drive free cash flow margins into the high-teens and high-single-digit FCF growth.

Discounted cash flow doesn’t suggest a lot of undervaluation now (mid-single-digit prospective returns), and that’s not unusual for “theme” stocks.

Turning to a multiples-based approach, I can argue for 17.5x forward multiple on the basis of the company’s profitability and above-average revenue growth potential, but “potential” is a tricky word and there will almost certainly be multiple compression if Hubbell comes up short on growth or margins. I’d also note that a 17.5x multiple doesn’t exactly support a robust premium at a fair value of around $485.

The Bottom Line

I want to like Hubbell more than I do. I think the revenue growth and margin leverage story is legit, and I think 2026 will see better results. I’ve also made it clear in the past that I really like electrification as a long-term theme, and I think Hubbell is a quality “picks and shovels” play. All of that said, valuation still matters, and I just can’t get comfortable with the valuation/modeling gymnastics it would take to get to a truly exciting fair value today.

data.title: Things That Can't Go On Forever...
data.description:
If something cannot go on forever, it will stop." - Herb Stein

Howard Marks, a co-founder of Oaktree Capital Management put out a sage observation earlier this week.

AI is currently the subject of great enthusiasm. If that enthusiasm doesn’t produce a bubble conforming to the historical pattern, that will be a first. "

This is a view I have articulated a few times on these pages over the past twelve months. The stock market feels like a 1999 redux. Just like that era, a major technology paradigm shift is driving both the equity markets and the economy. Back at the turn of the 20th century, it was the birth of the internet, now it is the AI Revolution. This huge surge of technology related spending is boosting GDP growth to a large extent.

There are some major differences between 1999 and 2026, however. Back in 1999, the U.S. was the unquestioned and only global power on the planet. Russia was in shambles after losing the Cold War and the subsequent dissolution of the Soviet Union. Hundreds of millions of people in China were still mired in poverty, and the nation had not yet joined the WTO and subsequently became the world's workshop.

In 1999, the Baby Boomers, the largest demographic cohort in U.S. history, were in their prime earning years. This year, the oldest Baby Boomers turn 80 years old. Most are retired or close to retirement and passing from this Earth at an accelerating rate. Consumer spending was also less bifurcated. The top 10% of households by income now accounts for roughly 50% of all consumer spending.

From a fiscal perspective, 2026 and 1999 are like night and day. In 1999, the U.S. ran a small fiscal surplus, and the overall national debt was less than $6 trillion. This fiscal year the U.S. will run an approximate $2 trillion fiscal deficit again. Federal debt has surpassed the $38 trillion and the debt to GDP ratio has roughly doubled since 1999.

And unfortunately, the rest of the G-7 is also struggling with their debt loads and have lower GDP growth rates to boot. Not to mention even lower birth/replacement rates.

In addition to being powered by a major technology paradigm shift, 1999 and 2026 have this in common. A large portion of this surge in tech spending is being powered by what was called 'vendor financing' in the late 1990s. Of course, the spending powered by the likes of Cisco Systems (CSCO), Global Crossing and Lucent pales in comparison to the current vendor financing ecosystem.

OpenAI which should deliver approximately $14 billion in revenues in FY2025, and is bleeding cash, has nonetheless just over $1.4 trillion in commitments in future computing capacity from the likes of Oracle (ORCL). The company is going to have to raise a huge amount of additional capital at current cash burn rates to meet those commitments. This is why I recently articulated that a huge and successful IPO of OpenAI would be one of the most positive signposts I could envision happening in 2026.

So, despite much more favorable tailwinds across many fronts in 1999, the bubble still popped in early 2000. Before the Internet Bust meltdown finally bottomed in the late summer of 2002, the NASDAQ fell just over 80% from peak to trough. The S&P 500 lost over 40%.

And investors should acknowledge that are far as valuations go, we are firmly in the end of the Internet Boom territory. This doesn't mean all stocks are substantially overvalued, even some of the tech giants that have led this advance over the past three years.

Given the projected earnings and sales growth rates for Alphabet (GOOG), paying 31 times forward earnings doesn't put the stock in value or even GARP territory in my view. That said, there are plenty of names like Palantir Technologies Inc. (PLTR) trading at 250 times forward earnings and nearly 100 times revenues, are practically screaming Bubble!

Let's take a look at some historical valuation metrics to compare the last innings of the Internet Boom in 1999 to the current equity market. They are very similar. One metric, the 'Buffett Indicator' shows today's market is substantially more overvalued than the tail end of the Internet Boom.

The Shiller PE ratio is flashing at least yellow right now as well.

In addition, unlike in generations past, almost all the gains from equity investments come from price appreciation. Little comes from dividend payments like in the past. This is why stock buybacks have become so important not only for the C-Suite making their EPS targets for equity and option awards, but for investors as well. Dividend yields also used to help to put floors under stock market declines.

Another cautionary reading is coming from corporate insiders, who are not stepping up to buy much in the market judging from the current insider buying/selling ratio.

I will end this look at market valuations, with the price to sales ratio of the S&P 500. This metric has only been higher at the tail end of the Internet Boom. This ratio is significantly higher than at the end of 2021. 2022 saw the S&P 500 fall just over 18% and the NASDAQ lose roughly one third of its value.

All this said, valuations are probably not going to be the trigger for the popping of the AI Narrative. Historically, valuations haven't been the pin that has pricked the bubble. However, extreme valuation levels do make the subsequent bear market much more painful whenever the inevitable occurs. In one of my next articles, I will try to tackle the most likely trigger for the eventual popping of the current market bubble.

In the meantime, the combination of tax cuts, tariff easing, and lower interest rates is likely going to push the market still higher. At least in the first half of 2026. The revision to GDP growth in Q4 earlier this week was certainly impressive. Therefore, I continue to hold 25% of my portfolio in short term treasuries and some cash to have 'dry powder' to deploy whenever the next 'hiccup' in the market occurs.

However, the rest of my portfolio is within covered call positions around the few stocks in the market that still sport reasonable valuations. Not an exciting configuration, but this strategy returned roughly 17% in both 2024 and 2025 for my portfolio, while taking much less risk than straight buy and hold investing.
